title: What Is Adrenaline? The Hormone That Makes Your Heart Race!
summary:
Have you ever felt your heart race before a big test, a performance, or even when you see your crush? That's adrenaline at work! Adrenaline (also called epinephrine) is a hormone — a chemical your body makes. It's part of your "fight-or-flight" response, which prepares you to deal with danger.

Here's how it works: When your brain senses a threat, it sends a signal to your adrenal glands (small organs near your kidneys). They release adrenaline into your blood. Then adrenaline makes your heart beat faster to pump blood to your muscles. It also makes you breathe faster, so more oxygen gets to your brain. That helps you think more clearly. It even slows down digestion to save energy for more important things.

This system evolved to help us escape from life-threatening dangers, like a wild animal. But today, everyday things can trigger it too — like taking a test, watching a scary movie, or falling in love. Sometimes adrenaline helps you focus and do your best. But other times, a racing heart and sweaty palms can make things harder. If you have too much adrenaline for a long time, it can damage your heart.

To calm down, try taking deep breaths, going for a walk, or talking to a friend. Adrenaline is also used as medicine — for example, in an EpiPen to help someone having a severe allergic reaction. So while adrenaline can be a little scary, it's also a superhero inside you!
why_it_matters: When you feel nervous before a test or a performance, it's just your body helping you stay alert — but knowing how to calm down is important.

keywords.explanation: Adrenaline is a chemical your body makes to help you react quickly in dangerous or exciting situations.
Epinephrine is another name for adrenaline, the body's 'get ready' chemical.
A hormone is a chemical that travels through your blood and tells parts of your body to do something.
Adrenal glands are small organs near your kidneys that release adrenaline.
An allergic reaction is when your body overreacts to something harmless, like pollen or peanuts, causing symptoms like swelling or trouble breathing.

Why does adrenaline slow down digestion?
options: To save energy for more important things
questions.correct_answer: To save energy for more important things

background_read: Hormones are like messengers in your body.
They travel through your blood to different parts and tell them what to do.
Article_Structure: WHO: Your body, specifically the adrenal glands.
WHAT: Adrenaline is a hormone that prepares you for danger or stress.
WHERE: In your body, starting from the adrenal glands near your kidneys.
WHY: To help you react quickly and stay safe in threatening situations.

perspectives.description: They use adrenaline as medicine in emergencies, like in EpiPens for allergic reactions.
They experience adrenaline often, and learning about it can help them manage stress.
They study adrenaline to understand how the body handles stress and to develop treatments.
